steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage is Victor's first-person account of scientific knowledge. Even the most learned philosopher could only dissect, name, and 'partially unveil' nature — causes beyond the first grade were 'utterly unknown.' Victor saw 'fortifications and impediments' blocking humans from nature's 'citadel' and 'repined' (felt frustrated). The question asks for the main idea.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Victor became disappointed in science when he realized that the practical knowledge laypeople have of nature is often deeper than the scientific understanding.: The passage says both the peasant and the philosopher knew little; it never claims laypeople know more than scientists. ✗
  • Choice B — Victor was impressed by the layperson's practical understanding of nature but thought science provided a clearer understanding.: Victor never credits science with clearer understanding — the whole point is that science also fails. ✗
  • Choice C — Victor was immediately impressed by the advantages of the scientific approach to understanding natural phenomena.: Victor is frustrated, not impressed; the text emphasizes science's inability to penetrate deeper causes. ✗
  • Choice D — Victor thinks that conventional scientific approaches to understanding natural phenomena will not yield the answers he seeks.: The 'fortifications,' the unknowable causes, and Victor's 'repining' all point to his belief that conventional science cannot reach nature's secrets. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
